title: Chapter 9: The Negotiation
content: The library at Fifth and Main was a 1920s Beaux-Arts building with marble floors, oak shelves, and the faint smell of old paper. It had survived the apocalypse mostly intact because nobody had thought to loot a library.
By the time Roxanne arrived, it was hers.

The purchase had been trivial — 500,000 Obols had appeared on the card, the building was hers, and Silas had spent the night arranging the main reading room into a proper meeting space. He'd even found candles.
"You lit candles?" Roxanne asked, stepping into the main hall.
"The electricity is unreliable. Candles are superior. They create atmosphere."
"You've been free for two days and you've already figured out interior design."
"I told you. I had time to think."
At exactly noon, the Coalition convoy arrived.

Colonel Voss was not what Roxanne expected. She'd imagined a military caricature — the kind of man who appointed himself "Colonel" because he owned a gun and a leather jacket. But Voss was older. Fifties. Gray hair. A face that had seen real hardship. He walked with a cane, but his eyes were sharp and calculating.
Behind him came Hayes and two other lieutenants. No visible weapons. Either a sign of trust or a sign that they didn't need them.
"Roxanne Vance," Voss said, stopping at the reading table. "You've made quite an impression in three days."
"I've had a busy three days."
"Buying a university. Clearing a territory. Negotiating a protection contract. Releasing a Primordial god from a four-billion-year seal."
He paused.
"That last one concerns me."
"Why?" Roxanne asked. "He's my employee now."
"He's a Primordial. The same class of being that created the Nether-Grid in the first place. You don't 'employ' something like that. You make a deal with it, and eventually it collects."
"Is that what you think you're doing here? Making a deal?"

Voss smiled. It was a thin, practiced expression. "I'm here to propose a mutually beneficial arrangement. The Salvation Coalition controls sixty percent of this city's infrastructure. We have the hospital, the water, the power. You have..."
He gestured at the card on the table in front of Roxanne.
"...purchasing power. Combined, we could stabilize the entire eastern seaboard."
"Combined," Roxanne repeated. "Under whose leadership?"
"Joint leadership. A council. You'd have a seat."
"A seat. One seat. On a council that you control."
"It's a starting position. You're young. You're new to this world. Experience counts."
Roxanne leaned back in her chair. Silas stood silently against the wall behind her, his red eyes fixed on Voss with an expression that was impossible to read.

"Let me tell you what I see, Colonel. I see a man who built an organization on scarcity. You control resources, so you control people. You decide who eats, who gets medicine, who gets protection. That's power."
She tapped the card.
"But I just broke your entire model. I have infinite resources. I can buy anything. I can protect anyone. Your scarcity-based power structure is obsolete, and you know it. That's why you're here."
"You're being naive," Voss said coldly. "Resources aren't power. Organization is power. Territory is power. People are power. You have a card. I have an army."
"You have six armed personnel and a pickup truck."
"I have two hundred and thirty-seven trained survivors under my command."
"And how many of them would follow you if I could offer them food, shelter, and safety without being asked to surrender their freedom?"
Voss's eyes narrowed.
"Let me make you a counter-offer," Roxanne said. "You keep the Coalition. You keep your territory, your command structure, your people. I don't want to run a city. I want to run a sanctuary."
"A sanctuary?"

"Blackwood University is a neutral zone. Anyone can come. Anyone can stay. No debts. No coercion. I'll provide protection, food, and housing. In exchange, the Coalition recognizes my territory as sovereign. No interference. No 'inspections.' No taxes."
"And what do I get?"
"A free clinic. Right here in the library. I'll buy medical supplies, equipment, and protection for it. Your people get healthcare without paying me anything. My people get the same."
Voss stared at her.
"You're offering to give me free healthcare."
"I'm offering to give everyone free healthcare. You just happen to be in a position to facilitate it."
"And if I refuse?"

"Then I buy the hospital. I buy the water treatment plant. I buy everything you control, one piece at a time, and I turn them all into neutral zones. Your people will come to me because I offer what you can't: safety without strings."
Voss was silent for a long moment.
Then he laughed.
It was a genuine laugh, rough and surprised.
"You're twenty-two years old. You've been in the apocalypse for three days. And you just out-negotiated me in my own city."
"I had a good teacher." She glanced at Silas. "And an infinite credit card."
Voss extended his hand.
"Deal. The clinic opens next week. My people will staff it. Your card supplies it. Neutral ground."
Roxanne shook his hand.
"Deal."

*End of Chapter 9*
\n---\n
After the Coalition convoy left, Lena found Roxanne still standing in the library's reading room, looking at the card.
"You just gave away a free clinic."
"I gave away funding for a clinic. The Coalition is staffing it. That's not giving away — that's a partnership."
"And the 278,000 Obols you spent on their debts?"
"An investment. They're not going to attack us now. They can't afford to lose the clinic. That's leverage."

Lena set down her sketchbook. "You've been in the apocalypse for three days. You've bought a university, a monster, and a god. You've negotiated a treaty with the local warlord. And you're talking about leverage."
"It's just common sense."
"It's not common sense. Common sense is hiding in a basement and hoping the monsters don't find you. What you're doing is... something else."
Roxanne looked at her friend.
"What am I doing?"
"I don't know. But I'm drawing it."

She held up her sketchbook. The page showed Roxanne standing in the library, the black card in her hand, silhouetted against a window of violet sky. She looked like a figure from a stained-glass window — caught between darkness and light, holding something that could destroy either.
"That's really good."
"I know."
"Can I keep it?"
"I'll frame it for your office."

The negotiation lasted two hours. By the end of it, Roxanne had secured: recognition of her territorial sovereignty, a promise of non-interference, access to the Coalition's water supply in exchange for medical supplies, and the establishment of a free clinic at the library.
Colonel Voss signed the agreement with a fountain pen that looked like it cost more than everything Roxanne had owned before the apocalypse.
"You're going to be trouble," he said as he capped the pen.
"I hope so."
"I don't mean it as a compliment."
"I'm taking it as one anyway."
She pocketed the signed contract. Walked out of the library. The violet sky had darkened to a deep bruise-purple, and the first eerie stars of the Nether-Grid night were beginning to appear.
Silas walked beside her. "You handled that well."
"I handled that adequately. Well would have been getting him to put his name on the clinic's funding."
"You got everything you actually needed. That's well."
She considered this.
"I guess I'm still calibrated to pre-apocalypse expectations. Back then, a good negotiation meant getting more than you asked for. Now it means getting enough to survive the week."
"Your calibration will adjust."
"How long does that take?"
"For most people? Years. For you?" He looked at her. "I suspect much less."
They walked back to campus under the alien sky.
